title: Web Storage
tldr: Web Storage provides two client-side storage mechanisms: localStorage (persists until explicitly deleted) and sessionStorage (cleared when the tab closes).
Both store key-value pairs where keys and values are strings. Use JSON.stringify() and JSON.parse() for objects/arrays.
Size limit: ~5-10 MB per origin. Data is origin-specific (protocol + domain + port) and never sent to the server.
laymanDefinition: Think of localStorage as a filing cabinet in your office that stays there permanently until you clean it out. sessionStorage is like a whiteboard in a meeting room — it's erased when the meeting ends and everyone leaves. Both are in your office (your browser), not shared with anyone else, and don't automatically communicate with the server. You can store notes (strings) in them. If you need to store more complex things like lists or objects, you first write them in a special format (JSON) and then store the message.

deepDive.heading: localStorage vs sessionStorage
deepDive.text: localStorage persists data across browser sessions — closing and reopening the browser keeps the data. sessionStorage is scoped to the tab: refreshing the page keeps data, but closing the tab or window clears it. Both are per-origin (https://example.com cannot access http://example.com data). sessionStorage is also per-tab — opening the same URL in a different tab creates a new sessionStorage.

deepDive.heading: Storage Events and Cross-Tab Communication
deepDive.text: When localStorage is modified in one tab, a storage event fires in all OTHER tabs/windows of the same origin. The event contains key, oldValue, newValue, url, and storageArea properties. This enables cross-tab communication. Note: the event does NOT fire in the tab that made the change. sessionStorage does not trigger storage events.

deepDive.heading: Storing Complex Data (Objects, Arrays)
deepDive.text: Web Storage only supports strings. To store objects or arrays, serialize with JSON.stringify() before setItem and parse with JSON.parse() after getItem. Be careful with circular references (they cause JSON.stringify to throw) and special types like Date (which becomes a string) or Map/Set (which lose their prototype).

deepDive.heading: Security Considerations
list: Web Storage is not secure for sensitive data — it's accessible via JavaScript (XSS vulnerable).
Data in Web Storage is not encrypted — never store passwords, tokens, or PII unless necessary.
Web Storage follows the same-origin policy — different subdomains cannot access each other's data.
Users can clear browser data at any time — don't rely on persistence for critical data.
Private/incognito browsing may limit storage (quota exhausted after closing).

deepDive.heading: Web Storage vs Cookies
deepDive.text: Cookies are sent with every HTTP request (affecting performance). Web Storage stays client-side only. Cookies have a 4KB size limit; Web Storage has 5-10MB. Cookies support the HttpOnly flag (not accessible via JS); Web Storage is always accessible via JS. Cookies can be scoped to paths/subdomains; Web Storage is per-origin. For storing client-only data, Web Storage is preferred.

interviewAnswer: Web Storage provides two client-side storage APIs: localStorage (persistent) and sessionStorage (tab-scoped). Both store string key-value pairs and have about 5-10MB per origin. Use setItem/getItem/removeItem/clear for operations. For objects, serialize with JSON.stringify and parse with JSON.parse. localStorage persists across browser sessions and triggers 'storage' events in other tabs. sessionStorage is cleared when the tab closes and is per-tab. Web Storage is synchronous and blocks the main thread, so avoid large reads/writes in critical paths. Never store sensitive data (tokens, PII) in Web Storage as it's accessible via XSS.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between localStorage and sessionStorage?
interviewQuestions.answer: localStorage persists until explicitly deleted — surviving browser restarts. sessionStorage is cleared when the tab or browser is closed. sessionStorage is also per-tab: opening the same URL in a new tab creates a fresh sessionStorage.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the storage limit for Web Storage?
interviewQuestions.answer: Typically 5-10 MB per origin. This varies by browser: Chrome/Opera ~10MB, Firefox ~5MB, Safari ~5MB. Exceeding the limit throws a QuotaExceededError.

interviewQuestions.question: How do you store objects in localStorage?
interviewQuestions.answer: Use JSON.stringify() before storing: localStorage.setItem('user', JSON.stringify({ name: 'Alice', age: 30 })). Use JSON.parse() when reading: const user = JSON.parse(localStorage.getItem('user')). Always handle parse errors with try/catch.

interviewQuestions.question: Can different subdomains access the same localStorage?
interviewQuestions.answer: No. Web Storage is per-origin (protocol + hostname + port). 'app.example.com' and 'api.example.com' have separate storage. Cookies can be shared across subdomains by setting domain=.example.com.

interviewQuestions.question: What happens when localStorage data exceeds the quota?
interviewQuestions.answer: A QuotaExceededError (or DOMException with code 22) is thrown. Catch it with try/catch: try { localStorage.setItem('key', val); } catch (e) { if (e.name === 'QuotaExceededError') { /* handle full storage */ } }

interviewQuestions.question: Does localStorage fire events in the same tab?
interviewQuestions.answer: No. The 'storage' event fires only in OTHER tabs/windows of the same origin, not in the tab that modified the data. This enables cross-tab synchronization.

interviewQuestions.question: Is Web Storage synchronous or asynchronous?
interviewQuestions.answer: Web Storage is synchronous and blocking. Reading or writing large amounts of data can block the main thread. For large data, consider IndexedDB (asynchronous, larger quotas). For simple key-value data under 5MB, Web Storage is fine.

interviewQuestions.question: Can you use Web Storage in private/incognito mode?
interviewQuestions.answer: Yes, but with limitations. Most browsers allow Web Storage in private mode, but the data is cleared when the private window closes. Some browsers (Safari) may have stricter limits or disable it entirely.

interviewQuestions.question: How do you check if a key exists in localStorage?
interviewQuestions.answer: Use getItem and check for null: if (localStorage.getItem('key') !== null) { /* exists */ }. The 'in' operator doesn't work because keys are not properties. Or use localStorage.hasOwnProperty('key').

interviewQuestions.question: What is the main security concern with Web Storage?
interviewQuestions.answer: Web Storage is accessible via JavaScript, making it vulnerable to XSS (Cross-Site Scripting). An attacker can read any data stored in localStorage/sessionStorage. Never store sensitive data like authentication tokens, passwords, or credit card numbers without additional protection (encryption, HttpOnly cookies).

codeExamples.title: Basic localStorage Operations
codeExamples.description: Basic CRUD operations for localStorage. All methods work identically for sessionStorage by replacing localStorage with sessionStorage.

codeExamples.title: Storing and Reading Objects
codeExamples.description: Always wrap JSON.parse in try/catch — stored data can become corrupted or be manually edited. Always handle quota errors on setItem.

codeExamples.title: sessionStorage for Tab-Scoped State
codeExamples.description: sessionStorage is perfect for form state preservation — data survives page refresh (useful if the page reloads) but is cleaned up when the tab closes.

codeExamples.title: Cross-Tab Synchronization with Storage Event
codeExamples.description: The 'storage' event enables cross-tab synchronization. When one tab modifies localStorage, all other tabs receive the change. Ideal for keeping settings in sync.

codeExamples.title: Storage-Safe Wrapper with Error Handling
codeExamples.description: A robust storage wrapper handles JSON serialization, quota errors, corrupt data, and cache eviction. Production code should use patterns like this.

mcqQuestions.question: What is the main difference between localStorage and sessionStorage?
options: localStorage persists; sessionStorage clears when the tab closes
mcqQuestions.explanation: localStorage persists until explicitly deleted. sessionStorage is cleared when the tab or browser window is closed.

mcqQuestions.question: Can you store a JavaScript object directly in localStorage?
options: No — only strings are supported. Use JSON.stringify/parse
Only if the object has no methods
mcqQuestions.explanation: Web Storage only supports string values. Objects must be serialized with JSON.stringify() and parsed with JSON.parse().

mcqQuestions.question: What is the typical size limit for localStorage?
mcqQuestions.explanation: The limit is approximately 5-10 MB per origin, varying by browser. Exceeding it throws QuotaExceededError.

mcqQuestions.question: When does the 'storage' event fire?
options: In the same tab that made the change
In all OTHER tabs of the same origin
In all tabs of any origin
mcqQuestions.explanation: The 'storage' event fires in all other tabs/windows of the same origin when localStorage is modified. The modifying tab does NOT receive the event.

mcqQuestions.question: How do you remove a single key from localStorage?
mcqQuestions.explanation: removeItem(key) removes a single key-value pair. clear() removes all data. delete localStorage[key] also works but is not standard.

mcqQuestions.question: Is Web Storage accessible across different subdomains?
options: Only if you set domain in the options
mcqQuestions.explanation: Web Storage follows the same-origin policy. 'app.example.com' and 'api.example.com' have separate storage.

mcqQuestions.question: What exception is thrown when localStorage is full?
mcqQuestions.explanation: QuotaExceededError (DOMException) is thrown when the storage quota is exceeded. Catch it with try/catch.

mcqQuestions.question: Which of the following is NOT a valid localStorage method?
mcqQuestions.explanation: The methods are getItem, setItem, removeItem, clear, and key(index). There is no 'storeItem' method.

mcqQuestions.question: Is Web Storage sent to the server with HTTP requests?
mcqQuestions.explanation: Web Storage data is never automatically sent to the server. This is a key difference from cookies, which are sent with every HTTP request.

mcqQuestions.question: Can Web Storage be used in private/incognito mode?
options: Yes, but data is cleared when the private window closes
mcqQuestions.explanation: Most browsers allow Web Storage in private mode but clear the data when the private window closes. Safari may impose stricter limits.
